task: I’ve got two newer people on a repetitive inspection step, and they keep missing the same couple of points even after a quick walkthrough. What’s a good way to tighten up the checklist and get them back on track without slowing the whole line down?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — the best way is to make the step **more visual, more fixed, and more repeatable**.

## Practical fix for repetitive inspection misses

### 1) Reduce the checklist to only the critical points
Keep only what actually matters:
- part ID / label
- orientation
- critical feature(s)
- obvious damage / contamination
- pass / hold / fail

If the checklist is too long, people start skipping.

### 2) Put the steps in the exact same order every time
Use a fixed sequence:
1. Identify the part
2. Compare to the known-good sample
3. Check the critical features
4. Say the result out loud
5. Record pass / hold / fail
6. Separate the part

No reordering. No jumping around.

### 3) Use a known-good example
Show them a good part or photo and have them compare only the features that matter:
- shape
- orientation
- markings
- surface condition
- critical dimensions or visual cues

### 4) Add a hard stop
If they:
- can’t verify it
- don’t see a match
- are unsure

then it goes to **hold**. No guessing.

### 5) Make them say the check out loud
That helps them slow down and actually inspect:
- “Part number matches.”
- “Orientation matches.”
- “Feature A is within spec.”
- “No visible damage.”

### 6) Fix the repeat miss immediately
If the same mistake keeps happening:
- point it out right away
- show the correct method
- have them recheck the next few parts
- keep watching until it sticks

### 7) Keep it to one page
Use:
- exact sequence
- photo/sample of the good part
- common mistakes
- hold rule
- who to call

## Simple rule
**If they keep missing the same step, the checklist is probably too broad or the check is not anchored to a known-good example.**

If you want, I can turn this into a short one-page floor checklist for new inspectors.

user_simulator.text:
Mostly right, but it’s a little too broad still. On the floor, I’d tighten it by tying each point to a specific defect they’ve already missed. Don’t just say “critical features” — list the actual two or three things that keep going wrong, with a photo or good part right there beside it.

I’d do it like this:
1. Part ID / label
2. The exact feature they keep missing
3. The exact visual cue for it
4. Pass / hold / fail
5. Initials and time

And if it’s a repetitive step, I’d add a short verification after the first 10 or 20 parts — not forever, just until they prove they’ve got it. That way you catch drift early without parking the line.

If you want, I can help turn it into a one-page checklist with the actual wording, clean and tight.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — that’s the right way to tighten it up.

## One-page floor checklist for repeat misses

### 1) Part ID
- Part number: __________
- Label matches?  Yes / No

### 2) Exact item they keep missing
List only the problem points:
- Feature 1: ____________________
- Feature 2: ____________________
- Feature 3: ____________________

### 3) Visual cue for each one
Write what they should actually look for:
- Feature 1 cue: ____________________
- Feature 2 cue: ____________________
- Feature 3 cue: ____________________

### 4) Decision
- **Pass** = matches the known-good sample
- **Hold** = not sure / can’t verify / mismatch
- **Fail** = confirmed defect

### 5) Record
- Initials: ____________________
- Time: ____________________

---

## Short verification step
For new people, add a quick check after the first **10–20 parts**:
- review a few inspected parts
- confirm the same defect points were checked correctly
- stop the extra check once they’re consistent

That gives you early catch without slowing the line all day.

## Best practice
Keep the **good part or photo right next to the checklist** so they can compare directly.

## Simple rule
**If they keep missing the same thing, name the exact miss, show the exact cue, and verify the first few runs until it sticks.**

If you want, I can draft the actual wording as a clean fill-in sheet.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s it. Tight enough to use on the floor.

The only thing I’d keep in mind is the trigger for the extra check — make it tied to the new hire or the repeat miss, not some blanket rule. Ten or twenty parts, then back off once they’re clean. No point babysitting it longer than needed.

The fill-in sheet format looks workable. Clean, one page, right next to the good part or photo. That’s the part that actually makes it stick.
